title: Proverbs 27
subtitle: Don't Boast of Tomorrow; Faithful Wounds; Iron Sharpens Iron; Know Your Flocks

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — Don't Boast of Tomorrow; Faithful Wounds; Iron Sharpens Iron

heb.paragraph: Do not boast about māḥār — tomorrow — for you do not know what a day may bring (v. 1). The word is simple but the theology is profound: the future belongs to God alone. Presumption about tomorrow is a form of functional atheism, behaving as if one's life is self-determined. James 4:13–16 develops this proverb directly.

Iron (barzel) sharpens iron, and one person sharpens another (v. 17). The metallurgical image is precise: iron is sharpened not by softer materials but only by something equally hard. Genuine friendship involves friction — comfortable relationships may not produce growth. The proverb valorises constructive conflict between equals.

panels.ctx: The first half of ch.27 contains three of Proverbs' most quoted relational sayings: the faithful wounds of a friend (v.6), iron sharpening iron (v.17), and the face-reflected-in-water metaphor for heart-knowledge through relationship (v.19).

cross.note: A friend loves at all times — the faithful-wounds proverb (v.6) specifies what that love sometimes requires: the wound that serves genuine good.
Walk with the wise and become wise — the iron-sharpens-iron principle of v.17 is the mechanism behind this companionship proverb.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Do not boast of tomorrow -- Alter notes this acknowledges human temporal limitation. The person who boasts of tomorrow treats the future as under their control.
Iron sharpens iron; one person sharpens another -- Proverbs' most positive statement about social wisdom formation. Sharpness comes from friction with other sharp people.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Do not boast about tomorrow -- epistemological humility about the future. Prohibition against presumption -- treating the future as already secured. Jas 4:13-15 applies directly.

Wounds from a friend can be trusted; an enemy multiplies kisses — Waltke notes the neemanin (trustworthy/reliable) quality of the friend's wounds is grounded in the friendship itself. The wound is trusted not because pain is good but because the one inflicting it has demonstrated consistent care for your flourishing. An enemy's kiss costs them nothing; a friend's rebuke costs them the relationship's comfort.

Calvin: Open rebuke is better than hidden love — the love that cannot bring itself to correct is not love but self-protection. To protect someone from uncomfortable truth in order to avoid your own discomfort is to prioritise your peace over their growth. Genuine love accepts the relational cost of honest correction.

Calvin: Iron sharpens iron — God uses the friction of genuine relationship to form the character of both parties. The metaphor is mechanical but the reality is personal: you become sharper through the resistance of engagement with another sharp person. Isolation produces dullness; genuine community produces sharpness.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring' connects to Jas 4:13-15 directly. The note traces the epistemological humility theme as consistent with Proverbs' overall recognition of human limits: we cannot fully know what a single day will bring, let alone plan for years ahead without covenantal trust.

The NET note on neemanin (trustworthy, reliable) characterises the friend's wounds as faithful even though they hurt. The same root gives amen (reliability, faithfulness). The note also discusses ataroth (many, excessive) for the enemy's kisses — the contrast is between painful reliability and pleasant unreliability. Wisdom chooses the former.

sections.header: Verses 13–27 — A Nagging Spouse; Know Your Flocks; Heart Shaped by What It Sees

heb.paragraph: A quarrelsome wife is like the dripping (deleq) of a leaky roof on a rainy day (v. 15). The image captures relentless, low-grade torment — not dramatic conflict but unceasing irritation. The sage adds that restraining her is like restraining the wind or grasping oil — futile. The observation is brutally honest about certain marital dysfunctions.

Know well the condition of your ṣōʾnekā — your flocks (v. 23). The closing agricultural passage (vv. 23–27) grounds wisdom in attentive stewardship. Riches do not last forever (v. 24), but the land, carefully tended, provides perpetually. The Hebrew word for 'know' (yādaʿ) implies intimate, detailed knowledge — not casual awareness but hands-on familiarity.

panels.ctx: The second half addresses household management — the prudent wife (v.15-16), knowing your flocks and herds (vv.23-27) — and the chapter's closing observation: riches do not endure forever, but tending what you have does. The chapter is the most relationally focused in Hezekiah's collection.

cross.note: Know the condition of your flocks and herds (v.23) — the agricultural management wisdom that connects to the 'eshet ḥayil who manages the household economy.
I have learned, in whatever state I am, to be content — the same contentment-through-diligent-management that vv.23-27 describe.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: As water reflects the face, so the heart reflects the person — the water-mirror image is one of Proverbs' most elegant metaphors. Alter notes still water provides a perfect, unsparing reflection: no flattery, no editing. So a person's life provides a perfect, unsparing reflection of the heart that produced it. Character is not hidden; it is visible in the life.

Know your flocks — the agricultural wisdom grounds the chapter's abstract relational observations in concrete management of tangible things. Alter notes wisdom is always practical before it is philosophical: the person who attends to the abstract while neglecting the actual has inverted wisdom's priorities.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: As water reflects the face, so one's life reflects the heart — Waltke notes the water-mirror image is Proverbs' most concentrated statement of the heart-life correspondence. The lev (heart) shapes the life; the life reveals the heart. No performance of character can be sustained indefinitely — eventually the heart's actual condition becomes visible in the life's pattern.

Know well the condition of your flocks — Waltke notes the agricultural coda grounds wisdom in the most basic economic reality: the wealth that sustains life requires attentive, ongoing stewardship. The person who knows the state of their flocks (what they actually have, what it needs) will sustain what they have; the person who does not will lose it.

Calvin: As water reflects the face, so the human heart reflects what the person truly is — character is not hidden. The life you live is the mirror of the heart you have. No pretence is ultimately sustainable: eventually the heart's orientation becomes visible in the pattern of choices, reactions, and relationships that constitute a life.

Calvin: Know the condition of your flocks — attend to the concrete responsibilities God has given. The person who attends to the abstract while neglecting the actual has inverted wisdom's priorities. Genuine responsibility begins with knowing what you actually have and what it actually needs.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on iron sharpens iron explains the mechanical metaphor for relational formation: the friction of genuine engagement with another person sharpens both parties. The note discusses how this applies to the wisdom tradition specifically: the student who engages a challenging teacher becomes sharper through the challenge; so does the teacher through the student's questions.

The NET note on 'know the condition of your flocks' grounds wisdom in concrete stewardship of what one actually has. The note traces the stewardship theme as consistent with Proverbs' practical orientation: wisdom manages what is actually in hand rather than speculating about what might be obtained.

ppl.name: The righteous
ppl.text: Integrity is both compass and shield.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"The wounds of a friend are faithful, but the kisses of an enemy are deceitful."NoteThe test of genuine friendship is not whether the other person makes you feel good but whether they are willing to wound you with truth when truth requires wounding.

src.title: Ancient Near Eastern wisdom
src.quote: These proverbs reflect Israel's engagement with international wisdom traditions, especially Egyptian.
src.note: Wisdom was a shared human enterprise across cultures.

src.title: Hezekian editorial work
src.quote: Proverbs 25:1 notes that men of Hezekiah copied these proverbs — one of the most historically specific attributions in Proverbs.
src.note: The editorial activity shows Proverbs as a living, curated collection.

rec.text: Proverbs 21-30 has been foundational to Christian virtue ethics — the practical outworking of the fear of the LORD.
These chapters have been widely cited in ethics, governance, and social teaching.

rows.label: Faithful Wounds
rows.text: Wounds from a friend can be trusted, but an enemy multiplies kisses. The friend who rebukes honestly is more trustworthy than the enemy who flatters. The painful truth from a proven friend is more valuable than the pleasant lie from a hidden adversary.

rows.label: Iron Sharpens Iron
rows.text: As iron sharpens iron, so one person sharpens another. Wisdom is formed in community, not in isolation. The friction of genuine engagement with another sharp person sharpens both parties.

tx.title: Chapter 27 — relatively stable transmission
tx.content: Proverbs 27 shows relatively minor divergence between MT and LXX compared to surrounding chapters. The LXX adds a few clarifying words but preserves the order and content of most proverbs. The chapter's agricultural imagery (vv. 23–27) is rendered faithfully across all witnesses.
tx.note: The agricultural passage's stability may reflect its practical, non-theological content — less subject to theological editing than passages about God, wisdom, or the afterlife.

debate.title: Faithful Wounds: What Makes Rebuke Loving?

positions.name: Love requires honesty even when it hurts
positions.argument: The wound from a friend is trustworthy because it serves the friend's actual flourishing rather than the comfort of both parties.

positions.name: Rebuke can be weaponised; discernment required
positions.argument: Not all rebuke is loving. The qualification is the rebuker's character and proven friendship.
